The Buckeyes played in two [Negro World Series](/wiki/Negro_World_Series "Negro World Series"), defeating the [Washington Homestead Grays](/wiki/Homestead_Grays "Homestead Grays") in 1945, and losing to the [New York Cubans](/wiki/New_York_Cubans "New York Cubans") in 1947. They were based in Cincinnati for their first season and Louisville for their second-to-last season.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Negro League Baseball team (1942–1948)

| Cleveland Buckeyes | |

The **Cleveland Buckeyes** were a [Negro league baseball](/wiki/Negro_league_baseball "Negro league baseball") team that played from 1942 to 1950 in the [Negro American League](/wiki/Negro_American_League "Negro American League"). The Buckeyes played in two [Negro World Series](/wiki/Negro_World_Series "Negro World Series"), defeating the [Washington Homestead Grays](/wiki/Homestead_Grays "Homestead Grays") in 1945, and losing to the [New York Cubans](/wiki/New_York_Cubans "New York Cubans") in 1947. They were based in Cincinnati for their first season and Louisville for their second-to-last season.

## Founding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Buckeyes&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Founding")]

For a list of annual win-loss records, see [List of Cleveland Buckeyes seasons](/wiki/List_of_Cleveland_Buckeyes_seasons "List of Cleveland Buckeyes seasons").

The Buckeyes were established in 1942 as the **Buckeyes Baseball Club**,[[1]](#cite_note-1) established initially in [Cincinnati](/wiki/Cincinnati,_Ohio "Cincinnati, Ohio"), [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio "Ohio")[[2]](#cite_note-Encyclopedia_of_Cleveland_History-2) (Ohio being the [Buckeye](/wiki/Aesculus_glabra "Aesculus glabra") State), scheduling seven league games in Cincinnati and nine in Cleveland, plus another five in Meadville, Pennsylvania.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) The following season, owner Ernest White of [Erie](/wiki/Erie,_Pennsylvania "Erie, Pennsylvania") made [Cleveland](/wiki/Cleveland,_Ohio "Cleveland, Ohio") the team's home city, where they played their games at [League Park](/wiki/League_Park "League Park").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Glory years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Buckeyes&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Glory years")]

While in Cleveland, the team achieved great success, including winning a pair of [Negro American League](/wiki/Negro_American_League "Negro American League") championships (1945, 1947) and a [Negro League World Series](/wiki/Negro_League_World_Series "Negro League World Series") title in 1945.[[2]](#cite_note-Encyclopedia_of_Cleveland_History-2)

## Decline and demise

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Buckeyes&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Decline and demise")]

In 1949 the team moved again, to [Louisville, Kentucky](/wiki/Louisville,_Kentucky "Louisville, Kentucky"), but it was to no avail and the **Louisville Buckeyes** returned to Cleveland for the first two months of the 1950 season before disbanding.[[2]](#cite_note-Encyclopedia_of_Cleveland_History-2)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Players

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Buckeyes&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Players")]

For a more comprehensive list, see [Cleveland Buckeyes all-time roster](/wiki/Cleveland_Buckeyes_all-time_roster "Cleveland Buckeyes all-time roster").

## Home fields

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Buckeyes&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Home fields")]

The Buckeyes played their inaugural season's home games at [Crosley Field](/wiki/Crosley_Field "Crosley Field") in Cincinnati.[[7]](#cite_note-7) After one season, they moved upstate to Cleveland and played at [League Park](/wiki/League_Park "League Park") until their demise,[[8]](#cite_note-8) except for 1949 when they played at [Parkway Field](/wiki/Parkway_Field "Parkway Field") in Louisville, Kentucky.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## MLB throwback jerseys

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Buckeyes&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: MLB throwback jerseys")]

On May 20, 2006, in Cleveland, the [Pittsburgh Pirates](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Pirates "Pittsburgh Pirates") and [Cleveland Indians](/wiki/Cleveland_Indians "Cleveland Indians") honored the Negro league teams by wearing the uniforms of the [Homestead Grays](/wiki/Homestead_Grays "Homestead Grays") and the Cleveland Buckeyes, respectively, during an inter-league game, as well as displaying the names on the scoreboard. The Pirates won the game 9–6.

On April 25, 2015, in Detroit, the Cleveland Indians and [Detroit Tigers](/wiki/Detroit_Tigers "Detroit Tigers") wore the uniforms of the Cleveland Buckeyes and [Detroit Stars](/wiki/Detroit_Stars "Detroit Stars"), respectively.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Players' names were not displayed on the jerseys. Detroit won the game 4–1.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

On May 7, 2017, in Kansas City, the Cleveland Indians and [Kansas City Royals](/wiki/Kansas_City_Royals "Kansas City Royals") wore the uniforms of the Cleveland Buckeyes and [Kansas City Monarchs](/wiki/Kansas_City_Monarchs "Kansas City Monarchs"), respectively. Players' names were not displayed on the jerseys. Cleveland won the game 1–0.
